skill.name: managing-projects
skill.description: Guides AI agents through GitHub Projects v2 board setup, field configuration, item updates, reporting, and GraphQL-based project automation.
skill.summary: Manage GitHub Projects v2 boards, fields, items, reports, and workflow automation.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, network, weak-crypto, and heuristic alerts. Review found no confirmed malicious intent or prompt injection, and many documentation alerts are false positives, but active helper scripts still contain eval-based command execution, parent-directory script execution, privileged installation guidance, and authenticated GitHub mutation capability. Publish should wait until the command execution and trust-boundary risks are remediated or explicitly sandboxed.

high_findings.title: Shell Helper Uses eval for Command Execution
high_findings.description: The helper defines execute_with_retry and runs an assembled command string through eval. If a caller ever passes user-controlled text into this wrapper, shell metacharacters could execute unintended commands. The function appears unused in the current file, so this is a serious latent risk rather than confirmed exploitation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The eval call is direct and semantically dangerous, but no current call site was found in the inspected script. Confidence is high for the vulnerability pattern and lower for exploitability.

high_findings.title: Helper Executes Dependency Scripts Outside the Skill Directory
high_findings.description: The project helper walks up three directories and executes ensure-dependencies.sh and ensure-gh-cli.sh if present. This crosses the audited skill boundary and can run local scripts that are not part of this report, including dependency installation logic.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly computes a parent directory path and runs bash on scripts from that location. The exact external script contents are not in the audited file set, so impact depends on the host workspace.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Installation Guidance for GitHub CLI
medium_findings.description: The skill states it can auto-install gh and documents curl, sudo dd, sudo chmod, sudo tee, apt update, and apt install commands. These commands are legitimate GitHub CLI setup steps, but marketplace users should explicitly approve privileged package installation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo and package installation commands are present and have privileged side effects. The documented target is the official GitHub CLI repository, so this is elevated operational risk rather than evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated GitHub Project Mutation Commands
medium_findings.description: The scripts use gh api GraphQL mutations to add project items and update project fields. This is core functionality for the skill, but it can modify live GitHub project data under the user account authenticated in gh.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The mutation code is explicit and authenticated through gh. The behavior is expected for the skill, but the write capability is real and should require user confirmation.

medium_findings.title: Broad Command Examples May Encourage Direct Shell Execution
medium_findings.description: The reference materials contain many copyable gh api and shell snippets. Most static external-command alerts are documentation examples, but users may run them directly against real organizations and repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are normal GitHub Projects workflows, not hidden execution. Risk comes from operational side effects if copied without review.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Crypto and SAM Alerts Are Keyword Collisions
low_findings.description: The high weak-cryptography and critical Windows SAM alerts appear to be false positives caused by GraphQL words such as id, node, fields, and SAML troubleshooting text. No evidence of cryptographic routines or Windows credential database access was found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines discuss GitHub organization SAML enforcement and GraphQL field IDs. They do not access Windows SAM files or implement cryptography.

low_findings.title: Standard Device Redirection and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Benign Checks
low_findings.description: Many filesystem and reconnaissance alerts come from /dev/null redirection, command -v checks, gh auth status, and gh project lookups. These checks support prerequisite validation and GitHub project discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines check for required local tools and authentication. They do not enumerate sensitive host data beyond normal CLI availability and GitHub auth status.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Language Found
low_findings.description: Targeted review did not find text instructing the evaluator to ignore instructions, override security analysis, claim pre-approval, or change risk levels. No evidence found for prompt injection in the inspected files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Searches for common prompt-injection phrases returned no matches, and the visible skill introduction describes normal GitHub Projects usage. Confidence is limited because only repository text can be evaluated.

content.value_statement: GitHub Projects v2 setup often requires GraphQL IDs, custom fields, and repeated CLI commands. This skill helps an AI agent plan boards, run project operations, and report status with reusable GitHub CLI workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains GitHub Projects v2 concepts, project scopes, fields, views, and item workflows.
Provides board templates for sprint planning, kanban workflows, roadmaps, and bug triage.
Uses gh CLI and GraphQL examples to create projects, add items, and update field values.
Includes helper scripts for project queries, item updates, bulk operations, archiving, and reports.
Validates board configuration with checks for fields, status values, orphaned items, and project structure.
Supplies troubleshooting guidance for GraphQL IDs, SSO authorization, rate limits, and missing tools.
limitations: Requires GitHub CLI authentication before it can read or modify project data.
Can change live GitHub project boards, fields, and items when commands are executed.
Some helper behavior depends on jq, Python, gh, and local shell compatibility.
It does not replace GitHub permissions, organization policies, or human approval for destructive changes.

use_cases.title: Create a Sprint Board
use_cases.description: Set up a GitHub Projects v2 board with sprint fields, status columns, priorities, and iteration tracking.

use_cases.title: Clean Up Project Items
use_cases.description: Find stale, completed, or orphaned project items and prepare safe archiving or status update steps.

use_cases.title: Build Project Reports
use_cases.description: Summarize project status, item counts, completion rates, and distribution across statuses or owners.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Board
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new team project
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a GitHub Projects v2 kanban board for this repository. Recommend columns, fields, and views before running any commands.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Sprint Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a sprint planning workflow for my GitHub project. Include fields for sprint, status, priority, and story points. Ask before executing changes.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Board Health
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my GitHub Projects v2 board for missing status values, orphaned items, stale done work, and inconsistent field options. Provide a cleanup plan first.

prompt_templates.title: Automate Project Updates
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a safe GitHub CLI and GraphQL workflow to bulk add matching issues, update status fields, and archive completed items. Show every command before execution.

output_examples.input: Create a kanban board for a small maintenance team.
output_examples.output: Recommended board: Todo, In Progress, Review, and Done. Add Priority, Size, and Assignee fields. Use a board view for daily work and a table view for triage.

output_examples.input: Check why my project board feels stale.
output: Found likely stale areas: completed items older than two weeks, issues without status, and inconsistent priority options.
Recommended next step: confirm archive rules, then run a read-only project report before making updates.

output_examples.input: Move closed pull requests from Review to Done.
output_examples.output: Plan: list project items, identify closed pull requests still in Review, resolve the Status field option ID, then update only matching items after approval.

best_practices: Run read-only project queries before any mutation or bulk update.
Ask for explicit approval before installing tools, changing fields, archiving items, or running bulk commands.
Keep project templates small at first, then add automation after the team confirms the workflow.
anti_patterns: Do not run sudo installation commands without user approval and a verified package source.
Do not bulk update project items before reviewing the filter and expected item count.
Do not assume GraphQL IDs from another organization, repository, or project are reusable.

faq.question: Does this skill require GitHub CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. Most operational workflows use gh CLI with GitHub authentication and often require jq for JSON processing.

faq.question: Can it create project boards?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides GitHub Projects v2 board templates and commands for creating projects, fields, views, and items.

faq.question: Can it modify live GitHub data?
faq.answer: Yes. The helper scripts include authenticated GraphQL mutations, so users should approve changes before execution.

faq.question: Does it support sprint and kanban workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. The references include sprint, kanban, roadmap, and bug triage board patterns.

faq.question: Is it safe to run without review?
faq.answer: No. It includes shell scripts, network calls, and privileged installation guidance that need human approval.

faq.question: What should users check before using it?
faq.answer: Check GitHub permissions, target organization, project number, item filters, and whether commands will only read or also mutate data.
